g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing an exploded view of the Rover robot, where all individual components are separated but aligned according to their assembly positions.
The Image: This is a comprehensive disassembly reference. Every part—from the laser-cut wood to the electronic modules—is floating in 3D space, providing a clear map of how the robot breaks down into its fundamental inventory.
Key Component Groups:
The Chassis Core: The main wooden baseplate is central, with the two motor spacers and the motor holder floating above and below it.
Front Sensor Assembly: The ultrasonic range finder and its wooden holder are shown separated from the front of the baseplate.
Drive System: The two wheels (rims and tires), the two FM90 motors, and their respective mounting screws are pulled out to the sides.
Rear Support: The ball caster, its plastic housing, the two wooden spacers, and the #2-56 screws are shown as a vertical stack at the rear.
Electronics: The Motor Module and its associated cables are hovering above the baseplate.
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Reverse Logic: Think of this image as the "Zero State." Your goal in disassembly is to move every physical part on your desk to match its "floating" position here before returning it to the storage bin.
Hardware Sorting: As you begin removing screws, notice that they are different lengths. Keep the long #4-40 motor screws (C) separate from the shorter #4-40 holder screws (A) to make the next assembly easier.
Tension Release: The first step in a "soft" disassembly is often removing the O-ring from the front sensor. This releases the tension on the wooden holder and prevents the range finder from falling out unexpectedly while you are working on the heavier motor section.
Cable Safety: Always unplug the cables from the Motor Module before physically unmounting the motors or the sensor holder. This prevents the delicate copper strands inside the wires from being stretched or snapped.
g.title: Final Disassembly

text.tspan: Place each item on its spot on the Components sheet

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the first step of the Rover disassembly process.
The Action: The Motor Module circuit board is being lifted vertically away from the top of the wooden baseplate. The motor wires remain connected to the module but are being pulled up through the central hole in the chassis.
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points upward from the Velcro square on the baseplate to the underside of the Motor Module, indicating the separation of the hook-and-loop fastener.
Text Prompt: "Detach Motor Module from Base."
Warning Note: An exclamation icon in a circle is accompanied by the text: "Do not remove Velcro Pieces."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Separating the Velcro: To avoid putting stress on the circuit board, place one hand on the wooden base and use the other to gently peel the Motor Module upward. You should hear and feel the "crackle" of the Velcro releasing.
Cable Management: As you lift the board, the red and black wires will follow. Keep an eye on the 4-pin cable extending from the white port; ensure it isn't snagged on any other part of the frame as you move the module.
Preserving the Adhesive: Per the warning, leave the adhesive Velcro squares stuck to their respective surfaces (one on the wood, one on the PCB). This ensures they remain aligned for the next time the robot is assembled.
Connector Check: It is usually best to keep the motor wires plugged into the module for this step to keep them from falling back through the hole and getting tangled in the wheels.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 1A

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the electrical disconnection phase of the Rover disassembly.
The Action: The Motor Module is held above the chassis, and the two sets of red and black motor wires are being pulled away from their ports on the circuit board.
The Connection: Two orange arrows point away from the metal headers on the PCB toward the small plastic connectors at the end of the wires. This indicates that the connectors should be unplugged to completely free the electronics from the mechanical base.
Text Prompt: "Disconnect wires from Motor Module."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Grip the Connector: When unplugging the wires, always grasp the small plastic housing of the connector rather than pulling on the wires themselves. Pulling on the wires can cause them to fray or pull out of the crimped terminals.
Steady Support: Use one hand to hold the Motor Module steady and the other to unplug the wires. Since the board is only held up by the wires at this point, be careful not to drop it onto the wooden base once the last connection is broken.
Clear the Path: Once disconnected, the motor wires will naturally want to fall back through the central hole in the baseplate. You can let them hang there for now, as you will be unmounting the motors in a later step.
Storage: Now that the Motor Module is fully detached and disconnected, place it in a safe spot (like a small bin or a magnetic tray) so the pins on the bottom don't get bent.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 1B

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of hardware from the Range Finder Holder as part of the disassembly process.
The Action: Two #4-40 screws (labeled "A") are being withdrawn downward from the bottom of the Rover baseplate. Simultaneously, two matching #4-40 hex nuts are shown moving away from the T-shaped slots in the wooden holder.
The Connection: Orange dashed arrows point downward from the screw heads, indicating their removal. Another set of dashed arrows shows the nuts being pulled horizontally out of the captive T-slots on the front of the holder.
Text Prompt: "Remove Screws and Nut".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Securing the Hardware: As you loosen the #4-40 screws from the underside, the hex nuts inside the wooden slots may become loose and fall out. Hold the robot over a tray or use a finger to keep the nuts from dropping and rolling away.
Hardware Identification: These are the #4-40 Screws (A). Keep them together with the two #4-40 Nuts in your storage bin to ensure they aren't confused with the longer motor screws later.
Holder Stability: Once these two screws are removed, the Range Finder and its wooden holder are no longer bolted to the baseplate. They are only held in place by a single wooden tab at the bottom, so handle the chassis carefully to prevent the assembly from tipping over.
Tool Usage: You will likely need a screwdriver to fully back the screws out of the baseplate if they were tightened firmly during assembly.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 2A

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of the Range Finder and its wooden holder from the Rover's chassis.
The Action: The wooden Range Finder Holder, with the ultrasonic sensor still attached by an O-ring, is being lifted vertically out of the Rover baseplate.
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points upward from a square notch in the front of the wooden baseplate toward the bottom tab of the holder. This indicates that after the screws and nuts were removed in the previous step, the entire assembly can now be pulled straight up and away from the chassis.
Text Prompt: "Detach Holder from Rover Base".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Tab Clearance: The holder is secured to the baseplate by a small wooden "tongue" that fits into a square "groove." Pull straight up to ensure the wood does not bind or splinter as the tab clears the base.
Sensor Safety: Since the Range Finder sensor is still attached to the wooden piece by the O-ring, handle the assembly by the wooden edges to avoid touching the delicate ultrasonic "eyes" or the pins on the back of the sensor board.
Cable Management: If you haven't already disconnected the 4-pin cable from the sensor, do so gently before moving the holder too far from the baseplate to prevent snagging.
Sub-Assembly Storage: You can keep the Range Finder attached to its wooden holder with the O-ring unless you need to return each individual part to its original inventory slot.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 2B

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the final stage of sensor disassembly for the Rover robot.
The Action: The black O-ring is shown being expanded and pulled away from the Range Finder sensor and its wooden holder.
The Connection: Orange dashed arrows point outward from the center of the O-ring, indicating it should be stretched to clear the wooden tabs and the sensor's ultrasonic "eyes." Once the tension is released, the sensor board and the wooden holder piece fall away from each other.
Text Prompt: "Remove O-Ring to detach Range Finder from Holder".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Pinch and Pull: Use your thumb and forefinger to catch the edge of the O-ring where it hooks into the side notches of the wooden holder. Pull it outward with enough force to clear the wood but be careful not to snap it.
Sensor Handling: Once the O-ring is removed, the Range Finder is no longer supported. Hold the sensor by the edges of its green circuit board to avoid leaving fingerprints or oils on the metallic ultrasonic transducers.
Inventory Check: After this step, you should have three separate items: one ultrasonic sensor, one laser-cut wooden holder, and one black O-ring.
Hardware Storage: Place the O-ring back in your hardware bin immediately. Because they are small and round, they can easily roll off a desk and be difficult to find on the floor.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 2C

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of the rear support assembly from the tail of the Rover baseplate.
The Action: The ball caster assembly is being lowered away from the wooden baseplate. Simultaneously, the metal or plastic ball is shown being pulled out of the plastic housing.
The Connection: Orange dashed arrows point downward, showing the two #2-56 Screws (B) being withdrawn from the baseplate. A solid orange arrow points downward from the ball, indicating its removal from the holder.
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Hardware Identification: These are the #2-56 Screws (B). They are noticeably thinner than the #4-40 screws used elsewhere on the Rover. Keep them paired with the plastic housing and the spacers to make the next build easier.
The "Ball Pop": To remove the ball, you may need to use a small tool or your thumb to press it firmly until it snaps past the retaining tabs of the plastic housing.
Spacer Stack: Once the screws are removed, the two wooden #2 Spacers will become loose. Be ready to catch them so they don't slide off the screws and get lost.
Thread Check: Since these screws thread directly into the wooden baseplate, turn them slowly while removing them to avoid stripping the wood fibers.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 3

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of the wheels from the motors.
The Action: The wheel assembly (consisting of the rim and the rubber tire) is being pulled horizontally away from the white motor output shaft. A small screw, labeled "D", is shown being withdrawn from the center of the wheel rim.
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points from the center of the rim outward, indicating the direction the wheel should slide off the shaft. A small solid orange arrow points to the right, showing screw (D) being backed out of its locked position.
Text Prompt: "Detach Wheels from the Motors by removing Screws".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Remove Screw First: Use your screwdriver to completely remove Small Screw (D) from the center of each wheel. These screws are what lock the wheels onto the ridged motor shafts; the wheels will not move until these are out.
Storage Trick: After the wheel is removed, you can lightly thread screw (D) back into the motor shaft or place it in a small magnetic tray. This prevents these tiny screws from rolling off your workspace.
Firm Grip: Grasp the plastic spokes of the wheel and pull straight away from the Rover body. If it feels stuck, do not pry it with a tool; instead, wiggle it slightly side-to-side to help the internal plastic ridges clear the motor shaft.
Tire Maintenance: If the rubber tires are dusty or loose, this is a great time to peel them off the plastic rims, wipe them down, and stretch them back on to ensure a tight fit for the next build.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 4

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the unmounting of the geared motors and their support hardware.
The Action: The two Geared Motors are being lifted vertically away from the wooden baseplate notches. Above them, the wooden Motor Holder is also being removed. Two long screws, labeled "C", are shown being withdrawn upward through the entire stack.
The Connection: Orange dashed arrows point upward from the baseplate, through the motors and the holder, following the path of the screws. This indicates that once the screws are removed, all three components (the two motors and the holder) are freed from the chassis.
Text Prompt: "Detach the Motors Holder and Motors by removing Screws".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Hardware Identification: These are the Long Screws (C). They are the longest screws in the kit because they must pass through the holder and both motors to reach the baseplate.
Wire Management: As you lift the motors, pull the red and black wires back through the central circular hole in the baseplate. Be gentle to ensure the small plastic connectors don't get snagged on the edge of the wood.
The "Motor Sandwich": Note how the motors sit snugly within the wooden notches of the baseplate. If they feel stuck, remove the screws entirely and gently rock the Motor Holder side-to-side to loosen the assembly.
Organization: Keep the Motor Holder and the two Long Screws (C) together. Since the motors are identical, they can be stored together.
g.title: Rover Disassembly Step 5
Rover Disassembly Step 6
g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the final stage of the Rover disassembly process to return the chassis to its original state.
The Action: The two laser-cut wooden Motor Spacers are being pulled downward and away from the rectangular notches in the main Rover baseplate.
The Connection: Four orange dashed arrows point downward from the baseplate notches toward the spacers, indicating that they should be slid vertically out of their friction-fit positions.
Text Prompt: "Remove Motor Spacers from Base".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Friction Fit: These spacers are designed to fit snugly within the wood notches. If they feel tight, gently rock them back and forth while pulling downward to ease them out without splintering the wood.
Inventory Verification: After this step, your baseplate should be completely bare. You should have two identical wooden spacers that look like small, notched "beams" ready for storage.
Final Cleanup: Take a moment to check the notches for any wood dust or debris. This ensures that when you or the next builder goes to reinsert them, the fit remains precise and secure.
Storage Placement: Place these spacers in the same compartment as the other laser-cut wooden pieces, such as the Range Finder Holder and Motor Holder, to keep the kit organized.

text.tspan: piece on its spot on the
